todayMission.title: Linux Fundamentals: Systemd and Service Lifecycle Management
todayMission.whyToday: Systemd is the init system and service manager for modern Linux distributions. Mastering it is critical for ensuring that application services run continuously, restart on failures, and log their outputs predictably.

objectives.description: Configure a custom Systemd unit file (`.service`) to daemonize a background worker script.
Set up automated restart parameters, delay intervals, and execution users for service security.
Manage service status, enable autostart on system boot, and query outputs using `systemctl` and `journalctl`.

resources.title: How to Write a Systemd Service

labs.title: Lab: Create a Python script `/opt/devops-worker/worker.py` that outputs system metrics to stdout every 10 seconds.
labs.instructions: Create a Python script `/opt/devops-worker/worker.py` that outputs system metrics to stdout every 10 seconds.

labs.title: Lab: Create a Systemd service unit at `/etc/systemd/system/devops-worker.service` to run the worker script securely as a non-root user.
labs.instructions: Create a Systemd service unit at `/etc/systemd/system/devops-worker.service` to run the worker script securely as a non-root user.

labs.title: Lab: Simulate worker process crashes and confirm Systemd automatically restarts the process based on unit config.
labs.instructions: Simulate worker process crashes and confirm Systemd automatically restarts the process based on unit config.

successCriteria: Custom service runs successfully under status `active (running)`.
Systemd automatically restarts the script within 5 seconds of a simulated manual kill signal.
Service logs are extracted and parsed successfully from `journalctl -u devops-worker.service`.
checkpointQuestions: What is the operational difference between service status states `inactive (dead)` and `failed`?
Which section of the unit file contains the autostart hook dependency configuration?
How does `journalctl` handle log rotation and what does the `-f` flag perform?
todayMission.proofOfWorkSpec: Submit the complete `/etc/systemd/system/devops-worker.service` unit configuration and a copy of the tail end of `journalctl` showing the automated crash recovery logs.

whatWentRight: Correctly explained how octal representation of file permissions maps to binary states.
Implemented robust error catching and exit code definitions in the log rotation backup script.
Successfully restricted group access to archive folders.
weakSpots: Forgot to specify execution permission flags on the backup runner script, resulting in manual execution failures.
Did not account for absolute paths when defining the target cron directory.
corrections: Make sure to test execution flags (`chmod +x`) before submitting scripts.
Always write absolute paths in helper scripts scheduled via cron processes.

labs.name: Custom Systemd Daemon Setup
labs.description: Configure, register, and troubleshoot a background Daemon configured for auto-recovery.
successCriteria: Unit custom service file dummy-api.service running local python API.
Service automatically restarts on SIGKILL signal within 5 seconds.
Custom service logs output to syslog/journald correctly.
proofOfWorkRequired: journalctl logs showing SIGKILL crash and recovery events.

labs.name: Log Rotation and Cron Automation
labs.description: Build an automated shell backup task and configure system-level log rotation rules to avoid storage leaks.
successCriteria: Backup script executing via cron every midnight.
proofOfWorkRequired: Verified target files list showing rotated archives.

labs.name: Network Traffic Port Redirection
labs.description: Redirect inbound local interface traffic on Port 80 down to standard application Port 8080 using UFW or IPTables.
successCriteria: UFW routing table successfully forwarding traffic.
Verify connection works with client curl -I requests.
proofOfWorkRequired: Log showing raw port mapping response.

topicsCovered.title: Bash positional arguments and exit codes

strongAreas: Correctly explained how octal representation of file permissions maps to binary states.
Implemented robust error catching and exit code definitions in the log rotation backup script.
Successfully restricted group access to archive folders.
weakAreas: Forgot to specify execution permission flags on the backup runner script, resulting in manual execution failures.
Did not account for absolute paths when defining the target cron directory.
recommendedNextFocus: Make sure to test execution flags (`chmod +x`) before submitting scripts.
Always write absolute paths in helper scripts scheduled via cron processes.
reviews.strategy: Progress is solid. Permission mechanics and automation scripting are verified at operational level. Advance to Systemd Service Management.

strongAreas: Explained differences between soft links and hard links.
Found hidden configuration files inside system paths.
weakAreas: Shaky on understanding why the dynamic loader cache is in /var/run.
recommendedNextFocus: Study the volatile runtime memory directories.
reviews.strategy: Advance to permissions topic with focus on executable flag setups.
